title: Protecting PII in Cloud Services
description: This module addresses the principles and practices for protecting Personally Identifiable Information (PII) processed in cloud environments, as defined by ISO 27018. Participants will learn about cloud PII processing obligations, consent management, sub-processor governance, cross-border transfers, and breach notification requirements.

sections.title: Cloud PII Processing Principles
sections.content:
ISO 27018 establishes a set of principles specifically designed to protect PII processed by cloud service providers acting as PII processors. These principles build upon the broader data protection framework and address the unique risks and governance challenges inherent in cloud-based personal data processing.

The foundational principle is that cloud service providers must process PII only in accordance with the documented instructions of the PII controller. This means that the provider may not use customer PII for its own purposes, such as advertising, analytics, or product improvement, unless explicitly authorised by the controller and disclosed to the data subjects.

Transparency is a key requirement. Cloud service providers must be able to disclose to their customers the countries in which PII may be processed or stored, the identities of any sub-processors involved, and the specific security measures applied to protect PII. This information enables controllers to fulfil their own transparency obligations towards data subjects.

Accountability requires the cloud service provider to demonstrate compliance with its processing obligations through documented policies, procedures, and audit evidence. Regular independent assessments, such as SOC 2 or ISO 27018 certification audits, provide assurance to controllers that the provider's PII protection practices meet the required standard.

sections.title: Consent and Purpose Limitation in Cloud
sections.content:
When PII is processed in cloud environments, the principles of consent and purpose limitation take on additional dimensions. The PII controller retains responsibility for obtaining valid consent from data subjects, but must also ensure that the cloud service provider's processing activities remain within the bounds of that consent.

Cloud service providers must not process PII for purposes beyond those specified in the service agreement with the controller. This restriction extends to all ancillary processing activities, including logging, monitoring, troubleshooting, and service improvement. Where the provider requires access to PII for operational purposes, such as resolving a support ticket, this must be subject to strict controls and documented authorisation.

The controller must conduct due diligence to verify that the cloud provider's terms of service and privacy policies do not grant the provider rights to use customer PII in ways that would be incompatible with the consents obtained from data subjects. Any contractual terms that permit the provider to process PII for its own purposes should be identified and either removed or explicitly accepted by the controller with full knowledge of the implications.

Changes to the scope or purpose of processing in the cloud environment must be communicated to the controller in advance, allowing the controller to assess the impact on existing consents and, where necessary, to obtain additional consent from data subjects before the changes take effect.

sections.title: Sub-Processor Management
sections.content:
Cloud service providers frequently engage sub-processors to deliver components of their service, such as content delivery networks, data centre operators, or specialist support providers. ISO 27018 requires that the use of sub-processors be subject to rigorous governance to ensure that PII protection obligations flow through the entire processing chain.

The cloud service provider must obtain prior authorisation from the PII controller before engaging any sub-processor that will have access to PII. This authorisation may take the form of specific prior approval for each sub-processor or general written authorisation with an obligation to inform the controller of any intended changes, giving the controller the opportunity to object.

Contractual arrangements with sub-processors must impose data protection obligations that are no less stringent than those in the agreement between the controller and the primary cloud service provider. The provider retains full liability to the controller for the acts and omissions of its sub-processors, and must implement appropriate oversight mechanisms to monitor sub-processor compliance.

Organisations should maintain an up-to-date register of all sub-processors involved in the processing of their PII, including the nature of the processing performed, the geographic location, and the contractual safeguards in place. Regular reviews of the sub-processor register, combined with periodic audits or assurance assessments, help to ensure that PII protection standards are maintained throughout the supply chain.

sections.title: Cross-Border Data Transfers
sections.content:
Cloud computing inherently involves the potential for data to cross national borders, whether through the geographic distribution of data centres, the provision of remote support services, or the use of sub-processors in different jurisdictions. ISO 27018 requires that appropriate safeguards be in place wherever PII is transferred across borders.

The PII controller must ensure that any international transfer of personal data complies with the data protection legislation of the jurisdiction from which the data originates. For organisations subject to UK data protection law, this means verifying that the destination country benefits from an adequacy decision, or that appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses or binding corporate rules are in place.

Cloud service providers should provide clear and comprehensive information about the locations in which PII may be processed, including the locations of sub-processors. Where the provider offers the ability to restrict processing to specific geographic regions, the controller should configure these settings in accordance with its regulatory obligations and risk appetite.

Organisations should also consider the implications of government access requests in foreign jurisdictions. Some countries' laws may require cloud service providers to disclose customer data to government authorities in circumstances that may not be compatible with the data protection rights of the data subjects. A thorough legal and risk assessment should be conducted before committing PII to cloud services that involve processing in such jurisdictions.

sections.title: Breach Notification for Cloud PII
sections.content:
ISO 27018 places specific obligations on cloud service providers to notify PII controllers promptly in the event of a personal data breach. Given the scale and sensitivity of PII processed in cloud environments, timely and effective breach notification is critical to enabling controllers to fulfil their own regulatory obligations and to mitigate harm to affected data subjects.

The cloud service provider must notify the PII controller without undue delay upon becoming aware of any unauthorised access to, or loss, disclosure, or alteration of, PII. The notification should include sufficient detail to enable the controller to assess the nature and scope of the breach, the categories and approximate number of data subjects affected, and the likely consequences.

The provider should also communicate the measures it has taken or proposes to take to address the breach, including steps to contain the incident, recover affected data, and prevent recurrence. Where the full details of the breach are not available at the time of initial notification, the provider should supply information in phases as it becomes available.

PII controllers must, in turn, assess whether the breach triggers a notification obligation to the supervisory authority and, where the breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects, to the affected individuals directly. The contractual arrangements between controller and provider should clearly define the breach notification process, including timeframes, communication channels, and escalation procedures, to ensure that regulatory deadlines can be met.

title: Cloud PII Technical Controls
description: This module covers the technical security controls required to protect PII in cloud environments under ISO 27018. Participants will learn about encryption strategies, access control mechanisms, data masking and anonymisation techniques, and secure disposal procedures for cloud-hosted personal data.

sections.title: Encryption for PII at Rest and in Transit
sections.content:
Encryption is a fundamental technical control for protecting PII in cloud environments, providing a critical layer of defence against unauthorised access, interception, and data leakage. ISO 27018 requires that appropriate encryption measures be applied to PII both at rest and in transit.

Encryption at rest protects PII stored on cloud infrastructure, including databases, file storage, backups, and snapshots. Organisations should ensure that all storage volumes and data repositories containing PII are encrypted using industry-standard algorithms, such as AES-256. The choice between provider-managed encryption keys, customer-managed keys, or customer-provided keys should be determined by the organisation's risk assessment and regulatory requirements.

Encryption in transit protects PII as it moves between the cloud service and its users, between cloud services, and between cloud regions. Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.2 or above should be enforced for all communications involving PII, and organisations should verify that their cloud providers support and enforce strong cipher suites.

Key management is a critical aspect of any encryption strategy. Encryption keys must be generated, stored, rotated, and retired in accordance with a documented key management policy. Where customer-managed keys are used, the organisation must ensure that key storage is appropriately secured and that key rotation schedules are adhered to. The loss of encryption keys can render data permanently inaccessible, making robust backup and recovery procedures for key material equally important.

sections.title: Access Controls for PII
sections.content:
Access controls for PII in cloud environments must be designed to ensure that only authorised individuals and systems can access personal data, and only to the extent necessary for their designated purpose. ISO 27018 requires cloud service providers to implement technical measures that support the controller's access management requirements.

Role-based access control (RBAC) should be implemented to align access permissions with job functions. Access to PII should be granted on a need-to-know basis, and privileged access to systems and databases containing PII should be restricted to a minimal number of authorised personnel. Privileged access management solutions can provide additional controls, such as just-in-time access, session recording, and approval workflows.

Multi-factor authentication must be enforced for all accounts that have access to PII, with particular emphasis on administrative and service accounts. Service-to-service authentication should use secure mechanisms such as mutual TLS, API keys with appropriate rotation policies, or managed identity services provided by the cloud platform.

Access to PII must be logged and monitored continuously. Audit logs should capture the identity of the accessor, the time of access, the data accessed, and the action performed. Regular access reviews should be conducted to verify that permissions remain appropriate, and any anomalous access patterns should be investigated promptly. Automated alerting for bulk data access, access from unusual locations, or access outside normal business hours provides an important detective control layer.

sections.title: Data Masking and Anonymisation
sections.content:
Data masking and anonymisation are techniques used to reduce the identifiability of PII whilst preserving its utility for legitimate processing purposes. ISO 27018 encourages the use of these techniques as part of a defence-in-depth strategy for protecting personal data in cloud environments.

Data masking involves replacing all or part of a PII element with a surrogate value, rendering the data non-identifiable in a specific context. Static masking permanently replaces PII in non-production environments, such as development and testing, ensuring that real personal data is never exposed outside of production systems. Dynamic masking applies transformations in real time, allowing authorised users to see the original data whilst presenting masked values to others.

Anonymisation is a more rigorous process that irreversibly transforms PII such that the data subject can no longer be identified, directly or indirectly. Truly anonymised data falls outside the scope of data protection legislation, making it a valuable technique for analytics, research, and reporting purposes. However, achieving genuine anonymisation requires careful consideration of re-identification risks, including the availability of external datasets that could be used to link anonymised records back to individuals.

Pseudonymisation, which replaces direct identifiers with artificial keys whilst retaining the ability to re-identify the data using separately held mapping information, offers a middle ground between full identification and anonymisation. Pseudonymised data remains subject to data protection regulation but benefits from a reduced risk profile. Organisations should apply the most appropriate technique based on the specific use case, the sensitivity of the data, and the regulatory context.

sections.title: Secure Disposal of Cloud PII
sections.content:
The secure disposal of PII at the end of its retention period, or upon termination of the cloud service relationship, is a critical control for preventing unauthorised access to personal data that is no longer required. ISO 27018 requires cloud service providers to implement disposal procedures that render PII irrecoverable.

Cloud environments present unique disposal challenges due to the abstracted nature of storage, the use of shared physical infrastructure, and the potential for data to persist in backups, snapshots, caches, and log files. Organisations must ensure that their disposal requirements extend to all copies of PII, not just the primary data store.

Technical disposal methods in cloud environments include cryptographic erasure, whereby the encryption keys protecting the data are securely destroyed, rendering the encrypted data permanently inaccessible. This approach is particularly effective in cloud environments where physical destruction of storage media is not feasible. Where cryptographic erasure is used, the organisation must verify that the key management system supports secure and verifiable key destruction.

Contractual arrangements with cloud service providers should explicitly address data disposal obligations, including the methods to be used, the timeframe for completion, and the provision of disposal certificates or attestations. Organisations should periodically verify compliance with these obligations through audits or assurance assessments. A documented disposal procedure that covers all categories of PII, all storage locations, and all cloud services in use ensures that no personal data is inadvertently retained beyond its approved retention period.
